next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

By midwinter the old ferry terminal smelled less like abandonment than broth, saline, wet wool, and the hot-metal tang of space heaters working above their pay grade. Salt had eaten lacework into the ticket grilles. The terrazzo floor stayed cold no matter how many rugs Jo scavenged and taped down. But the place no longer sounded empty. It clicked and coughed and argued. Gabe had coaxed one strip of the dead departure board back to life, and every few minutes the plastic letters flipped from INTAKE to SUTURES or BEDS or PORT CLEANING with the same hard clatter that used to send commuters toward the bay. Mira liked the noise more than she trusted herself to admit. It meant people expected the room to go on existing.

She stood behind the old information counter with a clipboard, triage stamps hanging from a shoelace at her wrist, and watched the morning line organize itself into something almost civilized. Wet coats steamed by the doorway. A man in a rented loading frame tried not to limp on a knee that belonged to three different shifts of other people. A student in a bargain nightlife shell kept pressing two fingers to her throat because every time Jo laughed the borrowed larynx answered in a baritone she swore was not hers. Somebody had left a sack of turnips by the fee jar. Somebody else had left twelve dollars, two tram cards, and a zip bag of sterile clamps. The money tin and the produce crate sat side by side now. That was the cooperative in its purest form: enough cash to buy gauze, enough food that nobody fainted in line, never quite enough of either....
open_hooks_to_carry: Show why a legal win can produce immediate material disaster.
Transform the old ferry terminal into an emergency triage site through collective action.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he body if it can travel. Yes, I know that's not an easy sentence. Move."

Tavi had his own phone out now, scrolling, swearing, dialing. "Service boards are down. Whole bargain portal is stuttering. Mira, if this cascades, people are going to start showing up wherever they think repair still exists."

Blue Hour was gone. The back room had been smashed, the wetbench burned down to salvage metal. There was no wherever left, only whoever.

Mira stood on the arbitration steps with the tribunal order dampening in her hand and understood the shape of the victory. They had forced the city to hear Lin as a living claim on the public record. They had put deliberate creation where CommonFrame wanted defect language. They had stopped the factory for a moment.

And now every cheap body built to keep poor people moving through other people's economies was about to be left in the rain with its maintenance withheld, its legal status contested, and its users told to wait for process.

Lin stepped close enough that their shoulder touched Mira's arm under the coat. "What do we do."

Down in the street, a tram bell clanged and somebody cursed and the cameras kept rolling because civic catastrophe was always good footage if it arrived with enough faces.

Mira looked at the missed calls again, at the order, at the crowd already starting to sort itself into the people asking questions and the people asking where to go. Then she folded the damp paper once and put it in the evidence sleeve with the ledger.

"We answer," she said.

It was the only sentence in her mouth that did not feel like a lie.
state_deltas_required: CommonFrame's bankruptcy turns victory into a mass-care crisis by abandoning the bargain bodies already in circulation with no maintenance path and no legal clarity.
The struggle shifts from proving wrongdoing to keeping living overlap bodies and poor renters alive through system failure.
